event: Norwegian chieftain Ingolfr Arnarson establishes the first permanent Norse settlement at Reykjavik, traditionally marking the start of Iceland's settlement era.
Iceland's settlers found the Althing at Thingvellir, creating one of the world's oldest functioning parliamentary assemblies and the Icelandic Commonwealth.
The Althing peacefully adopts Christianity as the official religion while allowing private pagan worship to continue.
Isleifur Gizurarson becomes the first native bishop of Iceland, establishing the episcopal see at Skalholt.
Iceland's laws are written down for the first time as the Gragas legal code, replacing reliance on oral recitation.
The violent Sturlung Era of clan feuds begins, destabilizing the Commonwealth and inviting Norwegian intervention.
Icelandic chieftains swear allegiance to the Norwegian crown under the Old Covenant, ending the independent Commonwealth.
Iceland comes under Danish rule as Norway joins the Kalmar Union uniting the Scandinavian kingdoms.
The Black Death reaches Iceland, killing an estimated half of the population over the following two years.
Catholic bishop Jon Arason is executed, completing the Protestant Reformation and Lutheran establishment in Iceland.
Bishop Gudbrandur Thorlaksson publishes the first complete Icelandic translation of the Bible, helping preserve the language.
Denmark imposes a trade monopoly on Iceland, restricting commerce to Danish merchants and stifling the economy for nearly two centuries.
Barbary pirates raid Iceland's coasts in the Turkish Abductions, carrying hundreds of Icelanders into slavery in North Africa.
A smallpox epidemic sweeps Iceland, killing roughly a quarter to a third of the population.
The catastrophic Laki volcanic eruption causes the Mist Hardships famine, killing around a fifth of Icelanders and much of the livestock.
Denmark abolishes the trade monopoly and grants Reykjavik town charter rights, beginning its growth as a center of commerce.
Denmark abolishes the ancient Althing, transferring its functions to a new high court.
Danish adventurer Jorgen Jorgensen briefly seizes power in the Icelandic Revolution, declaring the island independent before being deposed.
King Christian VIII orders the re-establishment of the Althing as a consultative assembly in Reykjavik.
Denmark grants Iceland a constitution and limited home rule over its own finances on the thousandth anniversary of settlement.
Iceland gains home rule with its first resident minister, Hannes Hafstein, based in Reykjavik.
The University of Iceland is founded in Reykjavik, consolidating the nation's higher education.
Icelandic women gain the right to vote and stand in parliamentary elections.
The Act of Union makes Iceland a sovereign kingdom in personal union with Denmark, retaining shared monarchy and foreign affairs.
British forces occupy Iceland to forestall a German takeover after Germany invades Denmark in World War II.
The United States takes over the defense of Iceland from Britain, stationing troops there for the rest of the war.
Iceland dissolves its union with Denmark and proclaims an independent republic at Thingvellir, electing Sveinn Bjornsson as first president.
Iceland joins the United Nations as a member state.
Iceland becomes a founding member of NATO despite domestic protests, choosing collective defense without a standing army.
A defense agreement returns United States military forces to the Keflavik base to safeguard Iceland during the Cold War.
Iceland extends its fishing limits and clashes with Britain in the first of the Cod Wars over disputed waters.
A submarine volcanic eruption off the south coast creates the new island of Surtsey, a natural laboratory for studying colonization.
The Eldfell eruption on Heimaey forces the evacuation of the entire town of Vestmannaeyjar and partially buries it in lava.
The Third Cod War ends with Iceland securing a 200-nautical-mile exclusive fishing zone after near-conflict with Britain.
Vigdis Finnbogadottir is elected president, becoming the world's first democratically elected female head of state.
Reykjavik hosts the Reagan-Gorbachev summit, a pivotal Cold War meeting that advanced nuclear arms reduction talks.
Iceland joins the European Economic Area, gaining access to the European single market without full EU membership.
The United States closes its military base at Keflavik, ending a continuous troop presence dating to World War II.
Iceland's three major banks collapse in the global financial crisis, plunging the country into a severe economic crash.
Johanna Sigurdardottir becomes prime minister, the world's first openly gay head of government, amid post-crash political upheaval.
The eruption of Eyjafjallajokull spews an ash cloud that grounds air travel across much of Europe for days.
The Panama Papers leak forces Prime Minister Sigmundur David Gunnlaugsson to resign over offshore holdings.
Iceland's national football team makes its first-ever appearance at the FIFA World Cup in Russia.
A series of volcanic eruptions on the Reykjanes Peninsula prompts repeated evacuations of the fishing town of Grindavik.
